name: John Coltrane
description: Saxophonist and composer whose restless evolution — from hard bop through modal jazz to free improvisation — redefined the possibilities of the tenor and soprano saxophone.

name: Radiohead
description: Oxford five-piece whose arc from guitar-band anthems through electronic reinvention made them one of the most critically acclaimed acts of their generation.

name: Bonnie "Prince" Billy
description: Will Oldham's three-decade catalog — from the raw Palace Brothers recordings through the Bonnie 'Prince' Billy years — is one of American music's most singular and restless bodies of work.

name: Veronica Falls
description: London-Glasgow quartet whose jangly, reverb-drenched guitar pop threaded C86 sweetness with gothic undertones across two near-perfect albums before quietly disbanding.

name: Miles Davis
albums: 60+ total (27 essential studio + 7 key live highlighted)
description: The most restlessly innovative figure in jazz history, whose unbroken arc of reinvention — from bebop through cool jazz, modal jazz, post-bop, and fusion — redefined the music at every turn.

name: Pixies
description: The loud-quiet-loud architects of alternative rock, whose taut surrealist songs — fusing punk velocity with pop melody and surf guitar — detonated a blueprint that shaped the sound of the 1990s.

name: PJ Harvey
description: One of the most fearless and shape-shifting artists in rock, whose three-decade career — from raw blues-punk fury through gothic Americana, spectral piano ballads, and politically charged art-rock — is defined by constant reinvention.

name: Destroyer
description: Dan Bejar's shape-shifting vehicle for three decades of literate, self-referential art-rock — moving restlessly from lo-fi home recordings through glam, MIDI orchestration, soft-rock sophistication, and electronic abstraction — while maintaining one of indie music's most distinctive lyrical voices.
